extending-a-join-path.label: Extending a Join Path
extending-a-join-path.summary: Learn to extend a join path by adding one related table at a time without rewriting the query from scratch.

sketch0.title: Build a Join Path One Link at a Time

sketch1.title: Add a Table Without Changing the Meaning

try_extending_a_join_path_sketch0.title: Try Extending the Path to a Department ID
try_extending_a_join_path_sketch0.prompt: Extend the student-to-course join path so each row also includes the id of the department that owns the course. Return `students.name, courses.title, departments.id AS department_id` from `students` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `students.id = enrollments.student_id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `enrollments.course_id = courses.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`.
try_extending_a_join_path_sketch0.hint: Continue from `courses.department_id` to the matching department row.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `enrollments.course_id = courses.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`.

try_extending_a_join_path_sketch0.starterCode:
SELECT students.name, courses.title
FROM students
JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id
-- Add the departments table join here
try_extending_a_join_path_sketch0.solutionCode:
SELECT students.name,
       courses.title,
       departments.id AS department_id
FROM students
INNER JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
INNER JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id
INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id;

try_extending_a_join_path_sketch1.title: Try It: Include Term Information
try_extending_a_join_path_sketch1.prompt: Modify the query to also include the term information for each enrollment. Return `students.name, courses.title, departments.name AS department_name, enrollments.term` from `students` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `students.id = enrollments.student_id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `enrollments.course_id = courses.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`.
try_extending_a_join_path_sketch1.hint: Add one verified relationship at a time.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `enrollments.course_id = courses.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`.

try_extending_a_join_path_sketch1.starterCode:
SELECT students.name, courses.title, departments.name AS department_name
FROM students
JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id
JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id
-- Add term information to the SELECT clause
try_extending_a_join_path_sketch1.solutionCode:
SELECT students.name, courses.title, departments.name AS department_name, enrollments.term
FROM students
JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id
JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id;

single-choice-1.title: Understanding Many-to-Many Relationships
single-choice-1.prompt: Which table acts as a bridge in a many-to-many relationship between students and courses?
single-choice-1.hint: Add one verified relationship at a time.

help.concept: A longer join should extend an already-correct path by one documented relationship.
help.hint_1: Keep the existing student-enrollment-course joins unchanged.

single-choice-2.title: Identifying Join Conditions
single-choice-2.prompt: What is the correct join condition to connect the `courses` table to the `departments` table?
single-choice-2.hint: Add one verified relationship at a time.

help.concept: A longer join should extend an already-correct path by one documented relationship.
help.hint_1: Keep the existing student-enrollment-course joins unchanged.

multi-choice-1.title: Characteristics of a Bridge Table
multi-choice-1.prompt: Select all characteristics of a bridge table in a many-to-many relationship.
multi-choice-1.hint: Consider what makes a bridge table unique in its role.

help.concept: Bridge tables connect two tables in a many-to-many relationship.
help.hint_1: Bridge tables contain foreign keys referencing both related tables.
help.hint_2: They do not contain primary keys from the related tables themselves.

options.a: Contains foreign keys to both related tables
options.b: Directly contains data from both related tables
options.d: Contains primary keys from related tables

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Join Operations
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to correctly join the `students`, `enrollments`, `courses`, and `departments` tables.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: SQL Join Syntax
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the SQL statement to join `students` and `enrollments` on the correct condition: `JOIN enrollments ON [blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Identify the foreign key relationship between `students` and `enrollments`.

help.concept: The join condition should match the foreign key in `enrollments` to the primary key in `students`.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Adding a Column to SELECT
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: Complete the SQL statement to add the `term` column from `enrollments` to the SELECT clause: `SELECT students.name, courses.title, [blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: The `term` column is directly available from the `enrollments` table.

help.concept: Include additional columns in the SELECT clause to retrieve more data.
help.hint_1: The `term` column provides information about the enrollment period.
help.hint_2: Add `the blank` to the SELECT clause.

sketch-0.title: A join path is a chain of documented relationships
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
A **join path** is the ordered chain of tables that connects the values a report needs. When two requested columns do not live in neighboring tables, the path passes through every required relationship rather than jumping directly between the endpoints.

Student names and course titles are connected through `enrollments`:

```sql
SELECT students.name, courses.title
FROM students
INNER JOIN enrollments
  ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
INNER JOIN courses
  ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id;
```

First, the query follows `students.id → enrollments.student_id`. Next, it follows `enrollments.course_id → courses.id`. Each `ON` condition corresponds to one connector in the ERD. The sequence matters because the second relationship becomes available only after `enrollments` has joined the path.

Build longer joins the same way you would follow directions on a map: one verified turn at a time. After each join, ask what one row represents and whether the row count makes sense. Here, one row still represents one enrollment.

Writing the path explicitly makes debugging easier. If the result suddenly grows or loses rows, you can inspect the newest link instead of treating the whole query as one indivisible block.

sketch-1.title: Extend the path while protecting the result grain
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
A join path can be extended when the last table already contains a foreign key to the next table. Courses know their departments through `courses.department_id`, so the student-enrollment-course path can continue to `departments`.

```sql
SELECT students.name,
       courses.title,
       departments.name AS department_name
FROM students
INNER JOIN enrollments
  ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
INNER JOIN courses
  ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id
INNER JOIN departments
  ON courses.department_id = departments.id;
```

The new join adds a department name to each enrollment row. It does not create a new grain because every course points to one department. The result still describes one student-course enrollment, now enriched with the department that owns the course.

Before adding a table, answer three questions: **Which table already in the path holds the foreign key? Which primary key does it reference? What should one result row represent afterward?** Those answers determine the next `JOIN`, its `ON` condition, and whether the extension is safe.

If a later table has several matching rows, the path may multiply the current rows. That can be correct, but it should be predicted rather than discovered by surprise.
